title: Pricing Your First Product
situation: You have a working product with some early users or interest and need to set a price for the first time.
common_mistakes: Pricing based on what it cost you to build instead of the value it creates
Setting a low price to 'get users first' with no plan to raise it later
Anchoring to competitor pricing without understanding your positioning
Offering a free tier that cannibalizes your paid product
Avoiding the decision entirely by staying in beta forever
challenge_questions: What does your best potential customer currently pay — in money, time, or pain — to solve this problem without you?
If you charged 5x your current instinct, who would still buy? What does that tell you about your real market?
Are you pricing for validation (learning if people will pay at all) or for revenue (sustaining yourself)?
What happens to your business model if you can never raise this price?
Would you rather have 10 customers at $100/mo or 100 customers at $10/mo? Why?
decision_factors: Stage of validation — pre-revenue vs. post-revenue changes the calculus
Your personal runway — how long can you afford to optimize for learning vs. income?
Market type — is this a market that respects premium pricing or expects cheap/free?
Switching costs — how hard is it for customers to leave once they start?
Your time cost — a $10/mo product with high support needs can destroy you as a solo operator
anti_patterns: The Perpetual Beta: Using 'beta' as an excuse to avoid the pricing conversation entirely
The Race to Free: Giving it away because you're afraid of rejection, then resenting the users
The Competitor Mirror: Copying a funded competitor's price without their cost structure or scale

title: Building an Audience vs. Building a Product First
situation: You're trying to decide whether to spend your limited time creating content and building an audience, or heads-down building the product.
common_mistakes: Building in silence for months then launching to zero people
Spending all your time on content and never shipping anything
Treating audience-building as a prerequisite instead of a parallel track
Confusing social media followers with potential customers
Building an audience around your personality instead of a problem
challenge_questions: If you had 10,000 followers tomorrow, what would you sell them? If you don't know, the audience won't help.
Can you build in public in a way that naturally creates content — or are content and product truly competing for the same hours?
Who are the 10 specific people you'd want as your first customers? Could you just go talk to them directly instead of broadcasting?
What's your actual bottleneck right now — distribution or product-market fit?
Are you drawn to content because it feels productive but is actually a way to avoid the harder work of shipping?
decision_factors: Product type — some products require trust before purchase (content helps), others sell on utility alone
Your natural strengths — some people genuinely build better through public discourse
Time horizon — audience compounds but slowly; product revenue can start faster
Market density — can you reach your buyers through direct outreach, or do you need inbound?
Your emotional needs — building alone is isolating; content creates connection but can become a trap
anti_patterns: The Infinite Funnel: Building audience → newsletter → course → community without ever building the core product
The Silent Builder: 6 months of heads-down work on something nobody asked for
The Vanity Pivot: Choosing content because likes feel better than the ambiguity of product development

title: When to Leave Your Day Job
situation: You have a side project showing promise and you're trying to decide if and when to go full-time on it.
common_mistakes: Quitting too early on excitement before validating revenue
Waiting so long that you burn out trying to do both
Using a revenue number as the only trigger without considering trajectory
Ignoring the non-financial costs: health insurance, family stress, identity shift
Assuming you'll be 3x more productive full-time (you won't be — at least not immediately)
challenge_questions: What specific number or milestone would make this decision feel obvious? Why haven't you committed to that as your trigger?
If your side project revenue dropped 50% next month, could you still survive financially for 12 months?
What are you actually giving up — salary, identity, security, social connection, health benefits — and have you honestly priced each one?
Is the day job actively preventing progress, or is it a convenient excuse for why things aren't moving faster?
Have you talked to your family about this in concrete terms — not 'someday I want to' but 'here's the plan and timeline'?
decision_factors: Monthly recurring revenue vs. one-time sales — predictability matters more than total amount
Family obligations and burn rate — what's your real monthly nut?
Health insurance situation — especially if you have dependents
Golden handcuffs — unvested equity, upcoming bonus, pension considerations
Opportunity cost of staying — is the day job actively eroding your window?
anti_patterns: The Rage Quit: Leaving after a bad week at work and calling it entrepreneurship
The Endless Hedge: Doing both forever because the transition feels too risky
The Fantasy Math: 'If I just work full-time on this, revenue will 5x'

title: Choosing What to Build
situation: You have multiple ideas — or no ideas — and need to commit to building something specific.
common_mistakes: Evaluating ideas in your head instead of against real criteria
Choosing the most technically interesting idea instead of the most commercially viable one
Waiting for the 'perfect' idea that checks every box
Picking an idea because it worked for someone else without understanding the context
Choosing based on what you can build instead of what people will pay for
challenge_questions: Can you describe a specific person with this problem — not a demographic, but someone you've actually talked to?
Would you use this product yourself, every week, even if you weren't building it?
What's the simplest version you could charge money for within 30 days?
If this idea fails in 6 months, what will you have learned that's useful for the next thing?
Are you excited about the problem or the solution? Excitement about solutions fades; problems sustain you.
decision_factors: Personal domain expertise — can you build credibly in this space?
Market accessibility — can you reach buyers without a massive budget?
Revenue model clarity — is it obvious how this makes money?
Technical feasibility for one person — can you build and maintain this solo?
Your energy and curiosity — will you still care about this problem in 18 months?
anti_patterns: The Idea Collector: Maintaining a list of 50 ideas and adding to it instead of committing
The Clone Trap: 'Uber for X' or 'Notion but better' without a genuine insight
The Passion Fallacy: Choosing based purely on passion without checking if anyone will pay

title: Going Solo vs. Finding a Co-founder
situation: You're debating whether to find a co-founder or build alone.
common_mistakes: Finding a co-founder because you're lonely, not because you need complementary skills
Assuming solo means you can't build a big business
Splitting equity 50/50 with someone you've known for two weeks
Using 'I need a co-founder' as a reason to delay starting
Choosing a co-founder who agrees with everything you say
challenge_questions: What specific tasks are you unable or unwilling to do that a co-founder would handle? Could you hire for those instead?
Are you looking for a co-founder or a therapist? Building alone is hard, but the wrong co-founder is harder.
Have you actually tried building solo for 90 days, or is this a theoretical concern?
If your co-founder quit tomorrow, would the business survive? If not, that's a dependency, not a partnership.
What's your conflict resolution style? Co-founder disagreements are inevitable — how would you handle 'I want to pivot and they don't'?
decision_factors: Skill gaps — are they truly gaps, or just areas of lower confidence?
Accountability needs — some people genuinely cannot self-motivate; that's okay to acknowledge
Fundraising intentions — VCs have a strong co-founder bias
Business type — some businesses (deep tech, two-sided marketplaces) are genuinely hard to solo
Your track record — have you finished projects alone before?
anti_patterns: The Comfort Hire: Bringing on a friend so building feels less scary
The Skills Myth: 'I'm technical so I need a business person' (solo builders do both)
The Speed Trap: Assuming two people = twice as fast (coordination costs are real)

title: When to Ship vs. When to Keep Building
situation: You have a working product but keep finding reasons to add one more feature before launching.
common_mistakes: Treating launch as a single irreversible moment instead of a continuous process
Adding features to avoid the vulnerability of putting your work in front of people
Polishing the UI when you haven't validated the core value proposition
Comparing your MVP to an established competitor's mature product
Waiting until you're 'not embarrassed' — you should be slightly embarrassed
challenge_questions: If you showed this to a potential customer today, what's the worst thing that could happen? Is that actually bad, or just uncomfortable?
Which of your planned features are for users and which are for your own comfort?
Can a real person get real value from what exists right now? If yes, why are you still building?
What will you learn from launching that you can't learn from building?
Are you adding features because customers asked for them, or because you imagined they would?
decision_factors: Core value deliverable — does the current version solve the core problem?
Reputational risk — is your market forgiving of early products or not?
Learning velocity — will launching accelerate your learning?
Technical debt — are you building on foundations that will need to be torn down?
Competitive timing — is someone else about to ship something similar?
anti_patterns: The One More Thing Loop: 'Just one more feature and then I'll launch' repeated monthly
The Perfectionist Shield: Using quality standards as a psychological defense against rejection
The Invisible Launch: Shipping but not telling anyone, then concluding nobody wants it

title: Saying No to Customer Requests
situation: Customers are asking for features or customizations that would pull you away from your core product vision.
common_mistakes: Building everything your loudest customer asks for
Treating every feature request as equal regardless of who's asking
Saying yes to enterprise customization that turns your product into consulting
Ignoring all requests because 'Steve Jobs didn't listen to customers'
Building for the customer you have instead of the customer you want
challenge_questions: If you build this, will it attract more customers like the one asking — and do you want more customers like them?
Is this request revealing a real gap in your product, or is it one person's workflow preference?
What would you stop working on to build this? Is that trade-off worth it?
Can you solve the underlying problem differently than the customer is suggesting?
If you say no, will this customer leave? And is that actually a bad outcome?
decision_factors: Revenue concentration — if one customer is 40%+ of revenue, you have a consulting business, not a product
Request frequency — are multiple unrelated customers asking for the same thing?
Strategic alignment — does this pull you toward or away from your product vision?
Maintenance cost — every feature you build, you maintain forever as a solo operator
Expansion revenue potential — will this unlock a new tier or market?
anti_patterns: The Custom Shop: Saying yes to everything and becoming a bespoke development agency
The Ivory Tower: Dismissing all customer input as uninformed
The Revenue Panic: Building whatever the biggest check demands regardless of strategy

title: Recognizing and Addressing Burnout
situation: You're working constantly but feeling less productive, less excited, and increasingly resentful of the work.
common_mistakes: Interpreting burnout as laziness and pushing harder
Taking a weekend off and calling it recovery
Blaming the business model when the problem is pace and boundaries
Comparing your output to founders who have teams, funding, or no families
Believing that passion should make burnout impossible
challenge_questions: When was the last time you did something purely for enjoyment that wasn't building, learning, or optimizing?
If you took a full week off, what are you afraid would happen? Is that fear based on reality or anxiety?
Are you building a business that requires you to be unsustainable, or have you chosen to operate that way?
What would a sustainable version of your current schedule look like — not ideal, just sustainable?
Who in your life has told you you're overworking, and why did you dismiss them?
decision_factors: Physical symptoms — sleep disruption, appetite changes, persistent fatigue are red flags
Relationship strain — if the people closest to you are worried, believe them
Decision quality — burnout makes you worse at the decisions that matter most
Recovery time needed — the longer you wait, the longer recovery takes
Business resilience — can the business survive you operating at 70% for a month?
anti_patterns: The Martyr Complex: Wearing exhaustion as proof of commitment
The Optimization Trap: Trying to 'hack' your way out of burnout with productivity systems
The Comparison Spiral: 'They're working harder and they seem fine'

title: When to Pivot vs. When to Persist
situation: Things aren't working as expected and you're trying to decide whether to change direction or push through.
common_mistakes: Pivoting every time something gets hard instead of distinguishing hard from wrong
Persisting purely because of sunk cost — time already invested
Pivoting to something easier instead of something better
Using 'pivot' as a euphemism for 'start over with a new shiny idea'
Never defining what failure looks like, so you can never honestly evaluate progress
challenge_questions: What specific evidence would convince you this isn't working? Have you actually looked for that evidence, or are you avoiding it?
Is the problem with your product, your market, your distribution, or your execution? These require different responses.
If a friend described your exact situation, would you tell them to keep going?
What did you expect to be true by now that isn't? Was the expectation reasonable?
Are you bored or are you stuck? Boredom is a you-problem; stuck might be a market-problem.
decision_factors: Signal quality — are you getting clear 'no' signals or just silence?
Rate of learning — are you still learning something new each week?
Energy trajectory — is your motivation declining, stable, or recovering?
Market feedback — what are non-customers telling you about why they didn't buy?
Alternative quality — is the thing you'd pivot to genuinely better, or just different?
anti_patterns: The Pivot Addict: New idea every quarter, nothing gets past the prototype stage
The Sunk Cost Soldier: 'I've put in too much to quit now'
The Grass-Is-Greener Pivot: The new idea always looks better because it hasn't been tested yet

title: When to Use AI Tools vs. Do It Yourself
situation: You're deciding whether to use AI tools to accelerate work or build skills and understanding manually.
common_mistakes: Using AI to skip understanding and ending up unable to debug or iterate
Refusing to use AI tools out of pride and moving 10x slower than necessary
Treating AI output as final instead of as a starting draft
Using AI for everything including the parts that require your judgment and taste
Building on AI-generated code you don't understand in production systems
challenge_questions: What happens when this AI-generated code breaks at 2am and you need to fix it?
Is the time you're saving being reinvested in higher-value work, or just more volume?
Can you explain what this code does to someone else? If not, you don't own it yet.
Are you using AI as a force multiplier for skills you have, or as a substitute for skills you lack?
What's your plan when the AI tool changes its pricing, API, or capabilities?
decision_factors: Criticality — how bad is it if the AI output is wrong?
Your existing knowledge — AI amplifies expertise more than it replaces it
Iteration speed — will AI help you test faster or just produce more untested output?
Learning value — is this a skill you need to internalize or just a task to complete?
Dependency risk — are you building a skill or a dependency?
anti_patterns: The Vibes-Only Builder: Shipping AI-generated code without understanding any of it
The Purist: Refusing to use AI because 'real developers don't need it'
The Volume Trap: Using AI to produce 10x more mediocre output instead of 2x better output
